text: Welcome to this video of a robotic lower and middle lobectomy performed by Joel Dunning at James Cook University Hospital. This is the case of a lady who had this peripheral nodule in the right lower lobe at the CT scan with some important hilar lymphadenopathy. The PET scan showed hyperfixation in correspondence with the nodule and hilar lymphadenopathy along the bronchus intermedius

The surgeon carefully dissects within the oblique fissure, lifting the middle lobe to expose the pulmonary artery. The lower lobe is positioned at the bottom of the field. The bronchoscope light is visible inside the trachea, indicating its location behind the surgical area.

The surgeon divides the posterior oblique fissure, revealing the right main bronchus which branches into the upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius. A prominent vein descending to the lower lobe is dissected, displaying unusual anatomical positioning as it typically originates from the posterior segment of the upper lobe and extends to the apex of the lower lobe.

The surgeon divides the horizontal fissure and excises R-eleven lymph nodes located beneath an aberrant pulmonary vein. The dissection focuses on the pulmonary artery, identifying the middle lobe branches and another R-eleven lymph node. The aberrant vein is lifted to reveal a posterior ascending arterial branch.
The surgeon dissects the inferior pulmonary ligament and removes R9 lymph nodes. The pleura is then dissected posterior to the inferior pulmonary ligament along the bronchus intermedius.
The surgeon dissects between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius, encountering an abnormal vein with unclear anatomy. A small branch of the vein is cauterized.

The surgeon continues dissecting the R-eleven lymph node located between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius, revealing the arterial and venous anatomy. The dissection progresses to the 2R and 4R regions for lymphadenectomy, with the pleura being opened posterior to the superior vena cava and over the azygous vein.

The surgeon removes a large 4R lymph node packet using a glove finger technique. They return to the fissure to examine an aberrant vein originating from the posterior segment of the right upper lobe vein and descending to the apex of the lower lobe. Behind this vein, a posterior ascending branch to the upper lobe is visible, which the surgeon aims to preserve. The aberrant vessel is stapled, confirming it as a vein leading to the lower lobe.

The posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery is now clearly visible. The surgeon dissects additional R-XI lymph nodes adjacent to the upper lobe bronchus and some R-XII lymph nodes of the upper lobe. The middle lobe vein is identified and divided using a stapler.
The surgeon addresses a staple misfire, where the blade fails to reach the end of the tissue. The remaining branch is grasped with forceps and successfully divided using a second staple firing.

The surgeon uses a robotic arm to position a stapler around the lower lobe vein, dividing it successfully. The view then returns to the fissure, revealing the pulmonary artery. With the middle lobe vein already divided, the surgeon safely uses a stapler to complete the horizontal fissure.
The surgeon divides the lower lobe and middle lobe branches of the pulmonary artery using two separate firings, ensuring preservation of the posterior ascending branch to the upper lobe.

The surgeon staples the bronchus intermedius just below the right upper lobe bronchus under bronchoscopic vision, ensuring the inclusion of the right upper lobe takeoff. The lower and middle lobes are removed en bloc due to N-Y lymph nodes, placed in a plastic bag, and extracted through a low anterior assistant port.
The surgeon performs a meticulous check of the surgical field, ensuring all lymph nodes are removed, guided by PET scan findings.
